executiveSummary.text: Raytheon's successor entity benefits from substantial defence spending tailwinds and Pratt & Whitney's commercial aviation recovery, but the AGT1000 turbine defect issue and elevated P/E of 40.61x create a more nuanced risk-reward profile — HOLD.

businessModel.text: RTX (formed from the United Technologies and Raytheon merger) operates three divisions. Collins Aerospace Systems is a leading supplier of avionics, interiors, sensors and power systems for commercial and defence aviation. Pratt & Whitney manufactures jet engines for commercial and military aircraft, including the Geared Turbofan (GTF) and the F135 fighter engine. Raytheon Missiles & Defence supplies precision weapons, air defence systems (Patriot, Iron Dome in the US), sensors and advanced missiles to the US and allied governments.

recentCatalysts.text: The AGT1000 turbofan defect — involving metallic powder contamination in certain engine cohorts — has required a large number of shop visits and engine replacements, primarily affecting Airbus A320neo family aircraft. The commercial narrowbody market remains supply-constrained due to this issue and ongoing aircraft delivery ramp-up. On the defence side, RTX has been a primary beneficiary of increased NATO defence spending and US Army/USAF procurement modernisation budgets. The company won the next-generation Long Range Hypersonic Weapon (LRHW) prime contract.

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull Case (76): AGT1000 defect resolution is accelerated, shop visit rates normalise and Pratt & Whitney commercial engine volumes recover strongly. Defence orders accelerate: Patriot battery exports to NATO allies and Arrow 3 programmes. Collins defence content per platform drives above-market growth. EPS reaches $6.50+. Price: $235+.
            Base Case (63): AGT1000 remediation is ongoing but manageable; the commercial aftermarket grows as the A320neo fleet matures. Defence revenues grow mid-single digit on the back of continued US and allied procurement. Collins commercial aftermarket normalises post-pandemic. EPS grows to $5.50–$6.00. Price: $195–$220.
            Bear Case (46): AGT1000 defect proves more severe than modelled, requiring a larger-than-expected fleet grounding and materially higher remediation costs. Airbus delivery rates fall short, reducing Pratt & Whitney OEM volumes. Defence budgets tighten in a post-election fiscal consolidation. EPS falls to $4.50–$5.00. Price: below $165.
            Weighted Score: 64 — Bull 76 × 30% + Base 63 × 50% + Bear 46 × 20%

bull.scenario: Bull Case (76): AGT1000 defect resolution is accelerated, shop visit rates normalise and Pratt & Whitney commercial engine volumes recover strongly. Defence orders accelerate: Patriot battery exports to NATO allies and Arrow 3 programmes. Collins defence content per platform drives above-market growth. EPS reaches $6.50+. Price: $235+.

base.scenario: Base Case (63): AGT1000 remediation is ongoing but manageable; the commercial aftermarket grows as the A320neo fleet matures. Defence revenues grow mid-single digit on the back of continued US and allied procurement. Collins commercial aftermarket normalises post-pandemic. EPS grows to $5.50–$6.00. Price: $195–$220.

bear.scenario: Bear Case (46): AGT1000 defect proves more severe than modelled, requiring a larger-than-expected fleet grounding and materially higher remediation costs. Airbus delivery rates fall short, reducing Pratt & Whitney OEM volumes. Defence budgets tighten in a post-election fiscal consolidation. EPS falls to $4.50–$5.00. Price: below $165.

keyRisks.text: AGT1000 turbofan defect: The metallic powder contamination issue in certain AGT1000 engines has created a large and ongoing shop visit burden. If the root cause is more systemic than initially assesse

risks.risk: AGT1000 turbofan defect: The metallic powder contamination issue in certain AGT1000 engines has created a large and ongoing shop visit burden. If the root cause is more systemic than initially assessed, costs could be significantly higher and the fleet grounding more extensive.

Defence budget sensitivity: RTX is heavily reliant on US DoD procurement. Changes in administration, shifts in defence priorities (e.g. towards personnel vs. equipment) or fiscal constraints could affect Raytheon Missiles & Defence's order flow.
Elevated valuation: At 40.61x P/E, RTX is pricing in a relatively optimistic scenario for the Pratt & Whitney recovery and defence demand. The margin for error is limited.
Commercial aerospace cycle: Collins and Pratt & Whitney are exposed to the commercial aviation cycle. A demand slowdown or new aircraft programme delays could impair volumes.

whoShouldOwn.text: Owners: Defence sector investors who want broad Pratt & Whitney and Collins Aerospace exposure alongside Raytheon's missile and defence systems. Those who believe in the NATO spending up-cycle as a multi-year thesis. Avoiders: Valuation-sensitive investors. The current P/E of 40.61x leaves limited upside for a company with structural headwinds in its commercial aviation business.

recommendation.text:
HOLD. RTX is a high-quality defence prime with genuine exposure to the NATO spending up-cycle and commercial aerospace recovery. The valuation, however, prices in a relatively smooth resolution to the AGT1000 defect and full capture of defence tailwinds.
            Entry framework: BUY below $160 (approximately 30–32x base-case EPS — provides a margin of safety for the Pratt & Whitney uncertainty and higher discount rate given the cyclical commercial aerospace exposure). Reduce above $210 (36x+ P/E with meaningful execution risk).

entryExit.text: BUY below $160 (approximately 30–32x base-case EPS — provides a margin of safety for the Pratt & Whitney uncertainty and higher discount rate given the cyclical commercial aerospace exposure). Reduce above $210 (36x+ P/E with meaningful execution risk).

sources.marketData: Live quote data sourced via DYOR HQ data pipeline (Google Sheets + Yahoo Finance). Fields include price, market capitalisation, 52-week range, P/E, EPS, and volume.
sources.companyFilings: Public company filings, regulatory announcements, investor presentations, and RNS where referenced in the analysis.
